title: 12 Hidar — The Great Feast of Archangel Michael & The Assembly
narrative:
Hidar

Archangel Michael — The Great Feast of the Captain of the Host

St. Constantine the Great, Equal-to-the-Apostles (Monthly Commemoration)

St. John the Almsgiver, Patriarch of Alexandria

The twelfth day of Hidar is the Great Feast of the Archangel Michael — the highest solemnity the Ethiopian Church offers to the one whose name is both question and declaration: 'Who is like God?' The answer to the question is the content of the declaration: no one is like God, and Michael's name is the eternal assertion of the divine incomparability, the war-cry with which the heavenly hosts cast the ancient serpent from the courts of heaven, and the confession with which the assembled angels perpetually affirm the sovereignty they serve. Michael is the Commander of the Heavenly Hosts — the Sar haGadol, the Great Prince, the Captain of the Host of the Lord who appeared to Joshua before Jericho and said: 'Remove your sandals from your feet, for the place where you are standing is holy.' He is the Angel of Israel, the Guardian of the Church, the Intercessor for the Human Race — the one who, standing before the Throne that the Four Creatures support, carries the prayers of the faithful upward and the mercies of God downward, making himself the living bridge between the divine majesty and the human need.

The Ethiopian devotion to Michael exceeds that of almost every other Christian tradition in its depth, its intimacy, and its comprehensive scope. He is not only the warrior of the cosmic battle and the guardian of the national covenant — though he is both of these with a particularity the Ethiopian tradition holds precious. He is the friend of the dying, the consoler of the grieving, the healer of the sick, the defender of the oppressed. His church at Kulubi in the Harari highlands draws hundreds of thousands of pilgrims on his feast days each year, and the prayers offered there are the prayers of ordinary people in ordinary need: the mother whose child is ill, the farmer whose harvest has failed, the soldier whose courage is exhausted, the monk whose interior battle has gone on too long. Michael receives all of them without gradation, without hierarchy of urgency, because the Captain of the Host is also the Intercessor for the Human Race, and the two offices are one: the war he fights is always on behalf of the people he intercedes for, and the intercession he offers is always the extension of the battle's victory into the specific need of the individual petitioner.

St. Constantine the Great, in his monthly commemoration, stands beside Michael on the Great Feast day as the earthly mirror of the heavenly commission. Constantine's vision before the Milvian Bridge — 'In this sign, conquer' — was understood by the tradition as a revelation mediated through the angelic order, and Michael's protection of the Christian empire from that moment forward was regarded as the extension of his cosmic commission into the historical domain. The monthly Constantine on Michael's Great Feast day is the calendar's statement that the heavenly and earthly orders are not parallel but integrated — that Michael's lordship over the angelic host is the cosmic reality of which every Christian victory in the historical order is a terrestrial expression.

St. John the Almsgiver, the seventh-century Patriarch of Alexandria, grounds the Great Feast in the most concrete human register possible: a man whose episcopate was defined by the distribution of everything at his disposal to those in need. He gave away the episcopal treasury, sold himself into slavery to ransom others, fed the hungry of a city under siege, and lived with a simplicity so radical that his household staff complained. John the Almsgiver is Michael's human counterpart on the Great Feast day — the earthly intercessor for the human race, the one who carried the petitions not upward to the Throne but outward to the bread queue, who understood the intercessory office as the practical redistribution of what the divine mercy provides. Together, Michael and John the Almsgiver present the community with the feast's deepest truth: that the intercession which sustains the human race has both a heavenly form, in the archangel's ceaseless advocacy before the Throne, and an earthly form, in the patriarch's ceaseless distribution from the granary — and the two are the same mercy flowing in different directions from the same inexhaustible source.
